title: Insulating a basement or cellar
subtitle: Choosing between ceiling, wall, and floor insulation approaches
excerpt: A cold basement or cellar can pull heat from the floor above and introduce damp air into the living space. There are three approaches to insulating it: insulate the ceiling of the cellar to keep it cold, insulate the cellar walls and floor to bring it inside the thermal envelope, or a mix of both. The choice depends on how the cellar is used and whether damp is present.
sourceNotes: LABC technical guidance on basement conversions and insulation. BRE report on ground floors and basements (BR 443). NHBC Standards Chapter 5.4 on below-ground construction. Historic England guidance on damp in basements of older buildings.

glossaryTerms.definition: The insulated boundary of a heated building, separating the conditioned interior from the unheated exterior. Everything inside the thermal envelope benefits from heating. Bringing a cellar inside the envelope means insulating its walls and floor, not its ceiling.

A membrane with low vapour permeance installed on the warm side of insulation to limit the movement of water vapour into the insulation. Prevents condensation forming within the insulation layer. Abbreviated VCL.

content.text: Uninsulated cellars are cold in winter and often damp. The ceiling of the cellar (which is the ground floor of the house above) loses heat downward into the cellar void. This can account for 8 to 15 percent of a house's total heat loss. The fix depends on whether you want a cold cellar (the simpler option) or a warm, usable cellar.
Option 1: cold cellar (insulate the ceiling)
This keeps the cellar outside the
thermal envelope

. Insulation is fitted to the underside of the ground floor joists or concrete slab above. The cellar stays cold but the floor above it is warmer. This approach is simpler, cheaper, and works well if the cellar is unused or used only for cold storage.
For timber joist floors: fit 100 mm mineral wool slab or PIR board between the joists. Hold it up with netting, timber battens, or clips. Target U-value: 0.25 W/m²K or better.
For concrete slab: glue rigid PIR board to the underside of the slab with mechanical fixings. 80 mm PIR (lambda 0.022) gives a U-value of around 0.24 W/m²K.
Ventilation: the cellar must remain ventilated to prevent damp. Do not seal air bricks if insulating the ceiling.
Option 2: warm cellar (insulate the walls and floor)
This brings the cellar inside the thermal envelope. The cellar can be used as a habitable room. It requires more work and a careful approach to moisture.

Walls: the inside of the cellar walls can be dry-lined with PIR board bonded to masonry, or with a cavity drained membrane system plus studwork. Do not use standard plasterboard on a masonry basement wall without a damp-proof membrane behind it.
Floor: lay a damp-proof membrane on the existing floor, then rigid PIR board, then a screed or chipboard floating floor on top. 75 mm PIR below screed gives approximately 0.25 W/m²K.
Vapour control: a
vapour control layer
fits on the warm side of wall insulation. It stops moisture from the masonry getting into the insulation.
Damp: the critical check

Any active rising damp, penetrating damp, or groundwater ingress must be addressed before insulating. Trapping moisture inside insulation layers causes decay, mould, and structural damage. If the cellar floor or walls show white salt deposits (efflorescence), staining, or surface dampness, get a damp survey before proceeding.
When to call a professional

Damp treatment is almost always worth doing with a professional unless the cause is clearly surface condensation. Structural retaining walls should not be lined without advice. Any basement conversion (Option 2) that adds habitable floor area may require building regulations approval.
Worked example

A 1930s semi-detached house in Sheffield has a cellar covering 30 m² below the ground floor. The existing floor U-value is 0.8 W/m²K. Fitting 100 mm PIR to the cellar ceiling reduces this to 0.19 W/m²K. At a temperature difference of 10°C and 2,500 heating hours per year, the saving is approximately 1,825 kWh per year. At 6p/kWh gas, that is around 110 pounds per year. With materials costing around 600, pounds the simple payback is about five to six years.
